subject: nutrition
title: Specialized nutrition support
preambleMarkdown:
# Specialized nutrition support



> **Clinical-review notice:** This is a bullet-first examination and clinical-orientation draft. Local protocols, medication doses, procedural thresholds, pregnancy/pediatric pathways, emergency workflows, and jurisdiction-specific guidance must be checked before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Specialized nutrition support is a clinical condition or syndrome in nutrition.
- This article focuses on deficiency, excess, metabolism, counseling, refeeding risk, and special populations.
- The practical clinical priority is nutrition diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Start by deciding whether the presentation is stable, time-sensitive, complicated, recurrent, or incidental.
- Imported source notes are preserved below for clinical review and citation reconciliation.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify unstable, time-sensitive, or function-threatening presentations first.
- Anchor the assessment of Specialized nutrition support to the patient context: age, pregnancy status when relevant, immune status, comorbid disease, medications, prior episodes, and local resources.
- Use focused history and examination before broad testing.
- Choose investigations that change diagnosis, severity assessment, treatment, disposition, or follow-up.
- Treat reversible danger early and give clear safety-net instructions.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Specialized nutrition support is best defined by the specific clinical problem, anatomic site, mechanism, severity, and care setting.
- For this topic, the core concept is nutrition diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Separate suspected, confirmed, recurrent, complicated, and resolved disease states.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Acute, subacute, chronic, recurrent, or resolved state.
- Mild, moderate, severe, complicated, or life-threatening disease.
- Primary disease versus secondary complication or medication effect.
- Outpatient, emergency department, inpatient, ICU, pregnancy, pediatric, or older-adult setting.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The epidemiology of Specialized nutrition support depends on age, sex, geography, exposure, comorbid disease, medication use, pregnancy status when relevant, and ascertainment setting.
- In nutrition, exam questions usually test the common population plus the high-risk exception.
- Risk estimates should be updated from local data or current guideline sources before publication.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify the organ, tissue, vessel, nerve, compartment, joint, pathway, or system most directly involved in Specialized nutrition support.
- Map nearby structures that explain referred pain, spread, obstruction, ischemia, neurologic deficits, imaging findings, or procedure risk.
- For non-anatomic topics, focus on the structure most likely to change symptoms, testing, or treatment.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Define the normal function that Specialized nutrition support disrupts or depends on.
- Connect the physiology to symptoms, examination findings, laboratory abnormalities, imaging appearance, and treatment targets.
- Consider compensation, reserve, feedback loops, and decompensation triggers.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The central mechanism in Specialized nutrition support should explain why the patient develops the key symptoms and complications.
- Mechanisms may include obstruction, inflammation, ischemia, infection, immune injury, degeneration, trauma, thrombosis, endocrine imbalance, toxicity, neoplasia, or functional dysregulation.
- Distinguish the primary process from secondary complications and treatment-related harm.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider the common causes of Specialized nutrition support first, then actively check for dangerous but less common causes.
- Review congenital, acquired, infectious, inflammatory, traumatic, metabolic, neoplastic, medication-related, toxic, pregnancy-related, immune, and idiopathic causes when relevant.
- Use the time course and exposure history to narrow the cause.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Risk changes with age, family history, baseline disease, immune status, pregnancy, medications, procedures, travel, occupation, environment, and prior episodes.
- For Specialized nutrition support, risk factors should guide pretest probability and prevention, not replace objective assessment.
- Document modifiable risks that can be addressed during treatment or follow-up.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider inherited disease when presentation is early, familial, recurrent, syndromic, unusually severe, or associated with congenital findings.
- Ask about family history, ancestry when clinically relevant, consanguinity, pregnancy losses, and similar symptoms in relatives.
- Use genetic testing when results change diagnosis, screening, treatment, reproductive counseling, or family risk assessment.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider infection when fever, exposure, immunocompromise, procedure, device, wound, systemic inflammation, or rapid clinical decline is present.
- Identify the likely source, route of spread, organism group, need for cultures, need for isolation, and need for source control.
- Antimicrobial decisions should account for severity, host risk, allergies, renal or hepatic function, pregnancy status, local resistance, and prior cultures.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Symptoms and signs depend on acuity, organ system involved, severity, comorbidities, medications, and prior episodes.
- Classic findings help recognition but absence of a classic finding does not exclude serious disease.
- Complications may be the first presentation in older adults, immunocompromised patients, and patients with limited follow-up.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask onset, duration, progression, triggers, exposures, associated symptoms, prior episodes, and treatments already tried.
- Review medications, allergies, procedures, devices, travel, occupation, sexual history when relevant, pregnancy status when relevant, family history, and baseline function.
- For Specialized nutrition support, ask targeted questions that separate uncomplicated disease from dangerous complications.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Start with vital signs, mental status, perfusion, respiratory effort, hydration, pain severity, and overall appearance.
- Perform a focused nutrition examination that directly tests the suspected mechanism.
- Look for systemic involvement, complications, mimics, and findings that change urgency or disposition.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Shock, altered mental status, respiratory distress, severe hypoxemia, syncope, seizure, major bleeding, or rapidly progressive symptoms.
- Severe pain out of proportion, new neurologic deficit, threatened airway, sepsis, peritonitis, limb ischemia, compartment syndrome, or vision threat.
- Any Specialized nutrition support presentation with organ dysfunction, pregnancy risk, immunocompromise, failed initial treatment, or diagnostic uncertainty needs a lower threshold for escalation.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Compare Specialized nutrition support with disorders that share the same dominant symptom, anatomic site, exposure, laboratory pattern, or imaging abnormality.
- Prioritize dangerous mimics that would be harmed by delay or by the wrong treatment.
- Use acuity, location, risk factors, examination findings, labs, imaging, and response to treatment to narrow the differential.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- First decide whether Specialized nutrition support is unstable, complicated, recurrent, incidental, or safe for outpatient workup.
- Use the highest-yield initial test for the suspected syndrome rather than ordering broad panels without a decision point.
- If early tests are negative but suspicion remains high, reassess the history, repeat focused examination, and choose second-line testing based on the remaining differential.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use labs to assess severity, complications, treatment safety, and alternative diagnoses.
- Use imaging, bedside tests, cultures, tissue sampling, functional testing, or procedures when results change management.
- Interpret results with pretest probability and clinical context.
- Avoid tests that add risk, delay, or incidental findings without changing the next step.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnosis of Specialized nutrition support should combine compatible clinical pattern with accepted laboratory, imaging, procedural, histologic, physiologic, or scoring criteria when available.
- Do not diagnose from one nonspecific abnormality when the full syndrome does not fit.
- Document uncertainty and the follow-up plan when the diagnosis remains provisional.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Severity depends on stability, organ dysfunction, symptom burden, anatomic extent, complications, treatment risk, and ability to follow up.
- Identify who needs urgent referral, admission, ICU care, procedure, surgery, isolation, rehabilitation, or close outpatient review.
- Use validated disease-specific scoring or staging systems when they exist and are available locally.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Assess stability first, then treat the underlying cause and the complications already present.
- Use supportive care, medications, procedures, rehabilitation, and prevention according to severity and contraindications.
- Escalate to specialty care when diagnosis is uncertain, severe disease is present, or treatment risk is high.

### Initial Management

- Confirm whether Specialized nutrition support is stable, unstable, incidental, recurrent, or complicated.
- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, mental status, pain, hydration, perfusion, and immediate threats.
- Escalate early when red flags, organ dysfunction, procedural need, or diagnostic uncertainty is present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, observation, rehabilitation, isolation, procedural planning, or environmental change when it addresses nutrition diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Tailor activity, diet, wound, exposure, work, driving, return-to-play, or safety advice to the clinical setting.

### Pharmacological Management

- Use medication only when the indication is clear and contraindications have been checked.
- Verify dose, route, duration, interactions, allergies, renal and hepatic adjustment, pregnancy or lactation status, and monitoring.

### Procedures

- Use procedures when they confirm diagnosis, obtain tissue or fluid, provide source control, relieve obstruction, restore alignment, repair anatomy, or treat complications.
- Check consent, anatomy, imaging, bleeding risk, infection risk, sedation risk, equipment, and aftercare before proceeding.

### Surgical Management

- Surgical or procedural referral is needed for instability, failed medical management, obstruction, perforation, ischemia, uncontrolled bleeding, malignancy concern, repairable structural disease, or unsafe bedside management.
- Timing depends on severity, anatomy, operative risk, patient goals, and local expertise.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Track symptoms, vital signs, function, pain, intake/output when relevant, focused examination findings, key labs, adverse effects, and response to therapy.
- For Specialized nutrition support, monitoring should detect deterioration, treatment toxicity, recurrence, and missed complications.
- Escalate if expected improvement does not occur or new red flags appear.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm diagnosis, severity, treatment response, adherence, side effects, and patient understanding.
- Arrange repeat examination, labs, imaging, rehabilitation, surveillance, medication review, or specialty care when indicated.
- Give a clear reassessment interval and explicit return precautions.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Complications of Specialized nutrition support may be acute, chronic, recurrent, treatment-related, functional, psychosocial, or procedure-related.
- Prioritize complications that threaten life, airway, limb, vision, fertility, neurologic function, organ function, or public health.
- Document warning symptoms that require urgent reassessment.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Prognosis depends on cause, severity, timing of recognition, comorbid disease, age, treatment response, complications, and follow-up reliability.
- Early recognition of dangerous Specialized nutrition support patterns improves disposition and reduces preventable complications.
- Published prognostic statistics should be checked against current specialty sources before release.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Address modifiable risk factors, medication safety, vaccination or prophylaxis when relevant, exposure reduction, screening, and recurrence prevention.
- For Specialized nutrition support, prevention should target the cause, the complication pathway, and the follow-up failure points.
- Use patient-specific counseling rather than generic reassurance.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Explain Specialized nutrition support in plain language, including what is known, what remains uncertain, and what symptoms should trigger urgent care.
- Review medication purpose, expected benefit, side effects, duration, and what to do if symptoms worsen.
- Confirm the patient understands follow-up, restrictions, wound or device care when relevant, and return precautions.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Monitor vital signs, pain, function, mental status, intake/output when relevant, wounds or devices, and response to treatment.
- Escalate fever, bleeding, respiratory change, neurologic change, perfusion change, uncontrolled pain, medication reaction, or worsening function.
- Reinforce education, discharge instructions, safe mobility, adherence, and follow-up logistics.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy or lactation status, interactions, duplication, contraindications, adherence barriers, and monitoring needs.
- For Specialized nutrition support, medication review should include both treatment drugs and drugs that may cause, worsen, or mimic the problem.
- High-risk drugs require a documented indication, duration, monitoring plan, and counseling.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask about bleeding risk, anticoagulants, antiplatelets, immunosuppression, prosthetic material, active infection, and relevant medication adverse effects before invasive dental work.
- Coordinate with the treating clinician when medication interruption, prophylaxis, or infection control is being considered.
- Specialized nutrition support does not automatically imply dental antibiotic prophylaxis; use condition-specific indications and local guidance.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pediatrics: use age-specific normal values, developmental context, weight-based dosing, caregiver education, and safeguarding awareness.
- Pregnancy: stabilize the mother first, choose pregnancy-aware testing and medications when possible, and involve obstetrics for significant disease.
- Older adults: consider frailty, atypical presentation, falls, delirium, kidney function, polypharmacy, cognition, and goals of care.
- Renal or hepatic impairment: adjust diagnostic contrast, medications, dosing, and monitoring.
- Immunocompromised patients: lower the threshold for atypical infection, severe disease, cultures, imaging, admission, and interaction review.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current guidance for Specialized nutrition support may vary by country, specialty society, age group, pregnancy status, disease severity, and available resources.
- Compare local policy with major society guidance for diagnostic thresholds, treatment sequencing, drug choice, procedural timing, and follow-up intervals.
- Document where local practice differs from international guidance and why.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize first when the patient is unstable; diagnostic precision comes after immediate threats are controlled.
- Match each test to a decision it will change.
- Reassess after treatment because nonresponse is often a diagnostic clue.
- For Specialized nutrition support, the highest-yield clinical move is to identify severity and the next safe action.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Treating Specialized nutrition support as a label instead of confirming mechanism, severity, and complications.
- Ordering broad tests without a focused question.
- Missing red flags because the initial symptom sounds common.
- Ignoring pregnancy status, renal function, hepatic function, allergies, interactions, or follow-up barriers.
- Discharging without clear return precautions.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Step 1: know the mechanism, anatomy, microbiology, pharmacology, or physiology behind the topic.
- Step 2 CK: know the next best diagnostic or management step.
- Step 3: know disposition, monitoring, complications, follow-up, and patient safety.
- Board exams: know contraindications, high-risk exceptions, and when to escalate.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Know the classic presentation, first diagnostic step, first treatment step, dangerous complication, and follow-up need.
- Severity and stability decide disposition.
- Treatment should include prevention of recurrence when possible.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- This Specialized nutrition support draft still needs specialty review before publication.
- Medication doses, procedural thresholds, screening intervals, risk scores, and guideline claims should be localized.
- Imported notes, when present, should be reconciled with current evidence and cited sources.

sections.heading: References
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current specialty society guidance, local protocols, and institutional pathways should be checked during clinical review.
- When imported source notes are present, reconcile them against current references before publication.

### Source Excerpt: Nutritional Support

_Source lines: 44630-44742._

```text
Nutritional Support

A. Enteral Nutrition (administered into the GI tract)
1. Enteral nutrition is preferred over parenteral nutrition because the
intestine is used in a physiologic manner.

2. There are two methods of administering enteral nutrition.

a. Nasoenteric tubes (e.g., nasogastric or nasojejunal tubes)-best
for short-term nutrition.

b. Enterostomy tubes (e.g., PEG/G-tubes, J-tubes)-for long-term
support.

3. There are many different formulas available for tube feeds-special
formulas can be provided for patients with renal disease, liver
disease, diabetes mellitus, and so on.

4. Tube feeds can be delivered intermittently (in boluses) or
continuously.

a. Bolus (intermittent) feeding (into the stomach) requires close
nurse monitoring at initiation. There is no clear consensus on an
appropriate cutoff level for a safe gastric residual volume. There
is also no clear evidence that an elevated gastric residual
volume is associated with adverse clinical outcomes such as
mortality, pneumonia, or hospital length of stay. In general,
most nursing protocols require monitoring gastric residual
volumes every 6 to 8 hours. A high residual volume (usually
considered to be >500 mL) in a patient with elevated
aspiration risk may prompt holding of tube feeds temporarily.

However, this does not always necessitate stopping tube feeds.

b. Contrary to popular belief, the risk of aspiration pneumonia
does not differ between continuous pump feeding and
intermittent bolus feeding.

c. Postpyloric (duodenal or jejunal) feeding may decrease risk of
aspiration pneumonia in high-risk patients, when compared to
gastric feeding. There is limited evidence that one method is
superior to another in terms of clinical outcomes such as
mortality, length of stay, or duration of mechanical ventilation.

5. Complications of enteral nutrition.

a. Intolerance to tube feeds-check for diarrhea, constipation,
nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, or distention. If this occurs,
discuss with dietician and trial changing tube feed formulation.

b. Malpositioning of tube (in trachea/bronchus).

c. Aspiration pneumonia (the risk of aspiration is not lessened by
enteral feeding compared to oral feeding).

d. Overload of solutes-due to high rate of hyperosmolar feedings
(can cause diarrhea, electrolyte imbalance, hyperglycemia).

B. Parenteral Nutrition (administered into the vein)

1. The term TPN (total parenteral nutrition) is used for a high-
concentration solution that can be given alone to meet the body's
caloric demands. TPN can also play a supplementary role when
enteral feeding alone is inadequate
2. It is used if the patient cannot eat for prolonged periods or cannot
tolerate enteral feedings, and in severely malnourished patients
3. There are two formulations and methods of administration
a. Central-TPN administered via central venous catheter (e.g.,
subclavian vein)-preferred in patients who require long-term
support
b. Peripheral-PPN (peripheral parenteral nutrition) is
administered via peripheral line-one cannot administer as
much protein or calories with this as with a central line. It
should only be used for a short-term period. TPN cannot be
administered via peripheral line as its high osmotic load is not
well tolerated by peripheral veins.

4. Complications of parenteral nutrition
a. Electrolyte imbalances, volume disturbances
b. Hyperglycemia
c. Complications associated with placing a central line (e.g.,
pneumothorax)
d. Infection of central (or peripheral) line (higher rates of
infection, especially with fungal organisms, than usual due to
presence of nutrients and glucose)
Quick HIT
Best antibiotics for gram-positive cocci (Staph and Strep):
1. PRPs (oxacillin, nafcillin, etc.)

2. First-generation cephalosporins

## 3. Tetracyclines

Quick HIT
Hypersensitivity Reactions to Penicillin
- Type I hypersensitivity reaction.

- This may present with rash, angioedema, or even anaphylaxis.

- Cross-reactions with other beta-lactam antibiotics can occur.

- Nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea are not hypersensitivity
reactions.

- It may develop at any time, even if past treatments with
penicillin were uneventful.
```

<!-- source: lib.md import end -->

### Source Excerpt: Surgical Nutrition

_Source lines: 1209-1568._

```text
SURGICAL NUTRITION
I. Nutritional Assessment
A. History: weight loss, chronic illnesses (malignancy) , dietary habits, social habits
(predisposing to malnutrition) , and medications (that influence food intake)
B. Physical examination: loss of muscle or adipose tissue ; temporal wasting; organ
dysfunction; and changes in skin, hair, or neuromuscular function
C. Anthropometric data: weight change , triceps skin fold thickness, and midarm
circumference
D. Biochemical data: albumin, prealbumin, transferrin, total lymphocyte count, and
creatinine excretion. Albumin has a half-life of 20 days and therefore reflects an
indicator of long-term nutritional status and is a very important indicator of pre
operative morbidity and mortality. Prealbumin has a half-life of 2 to 3 days and
therefore reflects more acute changes in nutritional status.

II. Estimation of Nutritional Requirements
A. Energy requirements
l . Energy is needed for metabolic processes, core temperature maintenance, and
tissue repair.

2. Energy requirements can be estimated by indirect calorimetry and urinary
nitrogen excretion.

3. Basal energy expenditure may be estimated by the Harris-Benedict equation.

4. Fat contains 9 kcaVg, protein 4 kcaVg, carbohydrates 4 kcaVg, and dextrose
3.4 kcaVg.

B. Basic caloric need is 25 kcaVkglday. Most postsurgical patients have an energy
requirement of 30 kcaVkglday. If the degree of surgical stress increases secondary
to trauma or sepsis, this requirement goes up to 20% to 40% .

C. Protein requirements
l . Proteins are required for wound healing, and the minimum for a nonstressed
person is 1 gffknday.

2. A nonprotein calorie: nitrogen ratio of 1 5 0 : 1 is needed to prevent the utiliza
tion of protein as a source of energy.

3. In stress, more protein is required, so that a ratio of 90: 1 to 120: 1 is beneficial.

Ill. Nutritional Requirements in Specific Conditions
A. Renal failure
l . Renal failure results in an impaired ability to clear the byproducts of protein
metabolism.

## 2 . These patients are given nutrients in a restricted volume with great care not to

overfeed proteins.

## 3 . Administration of essential amino acids and high biologic value protein, such

as egg albumin, results in less frequent need for dialysis .

C e rtain n utrients are
especially important for
the Gl tract. G l utamine is
the most i m p o rtant fuel for
enterocytes. S h ort- c h a i n fatty
acids are the primary en ergy
source for c o l o n o cytes.

B. Hepatic failure
l . Liver damage and portosystemic shunting results in a derangement in the
level of amino acids in the blood, resulting in an increase in the aromatic to
branched-chain amino acids.

2. The aromatic amino acids are precursors of false neurotransmitters that con
tribute to hepatic encephalopathy.

3. Patients with hepatic failure are therefore given diets enriched in branched
chain amino acids and deHcient in aromatic amino acids.

C. Respiratory failure
l . Carbohydrate metabolism produces more C02 (respiratory quotient [ RQ] 1)
as compared to fat (RQ = 0 . 7) or protein (RQ = 0 . 8) .

2. Production of higher amounts of C02 results in more need for ventilatory
support.

3. It is therefore important to prevent overfeeding patients. The amount of carbo
hydrate intake can be reduced, and that of fat may be increased. However, this
must be done cautiously because high-fat diets may exacerbate lung injury.

D. Cardiac failure
l . Fluid overload may exacerbate cardiac failure.

## 2 . Concentrated solutions are therefore given to these patients to limit the

amount of fluid administered.

IV. Enteral Nutrition
A. General principles
l . Nutrition via the enteral route is preferred over the parenteral route, hence the
commonly used phrase in surgery patients, "if the gut works, use it. "

## 2 . Feeding the G I tract functions t o preserve the "gut mucosal barrier. " This bar

rier prevents the translocation of bacteria and bacterial toxins across the gut
into the host portal venous circulation.

3. Maintenance of the gut mucosal barrier requires (a) normal perfusion, (b) an
intact epithelium, and (c) normal mucosal immune mechanisms .

4. Luminal contact of food prevents intestinal mucosal atrophy and stimulates
intestinal production of immunoglobulin A (IgA) .

## 5 . Surgical patients who are adequately nourished and have not suffered a major

complication can tolerate 1 0 days of partial starvation before any significant
protein catabolism occurs . Therefore, most patients can be maintained on a
5% dextrose solution before return of feeding after surgery with no detrimental
outcome.

6. Initiation of enteral feeding should occur immediately after adequate
resuscitation.

B. Enteral formulas
l . Wide arrays of formulas are commercially available. The choice of an enteral
formula is influenced by the degree of organ dysfunction and nutrient needs.

2. Patients who have not been fed for a prolonged period of time are less likely to
tolerate complex solutions .

## 3 . Patients with malnutrition benefit from provision of dipeptides, tripeptides,

and medium-chain triglycerides because these substances are more easily
absorbed.

4. Major categories of enteral formulas are:
a. Low-residue isotonic formulas
( 1 ) These Hrst-line formulas for stable patients with an intact GI tract con
tain no Hber bulk and so leave minimal residue.

(2) They provide a caloric density of 1 kcaVmL and a nonprotein
calorie: nitrogen ratio of 1 5 0 : l .

b . Isotonic formulas with fiber
( 1 ) The formulas contain fiber, which delays intestinal transit time and
reduces the incidence of diarrhea.

(2) The Hber stimulates pancreatic lipase activity and is degraded by gut
bacteria into short-chain fatty acids.

S U R G I C A L P H Y S I O L O G Y e 1 7
c. Immune-enhancing formulas
( 1 ) The formulas contain special nutrients such as glutamine, arginine,
branched-chain amino acids , omega-3 fatty acids, nucleotides, and beta
carotene.

(2) The addition of amino acids generally doubles the amount of protein.

d. Calorie-dense formulas
( l ) The formulas provide 1 . 5 to 2 kcanmL and are therefore used in uid
restricted patients.

(2) They have a higher osmolarity than standard formulas and are therefore
used for intragastric feedings.

e. High-protein formulas
( 1 ) The formulas are used in critically ill patients with high protein
requirements.

(2) They provide nonprotein calorie:nitrogen ratios of 80: 1 to 1 20 : 1 .

f. Elemental formulas
( l ) The formulas contain predigested nutrients and are thus easy to absorb.

(2) They are deficient in fat, vitamins, and trace elements that limit their
long-term use. Instead, they are used in patients with malnutrition, gut
impairment, and pancreatitis.

g. Renal failure formulas
( l ) These formulas contain protein exclusively in the form of essential amino
acids and have a high nonprotein calorie:nitrogen ratio.

(2) They require lower uid volumes and contain lower concentrations of
potassium, magnesium, and phosphorus.

h. Pulmonary failure formulas
( l ) These formulas have a reduced content of carbohydrate and a
corresponding increased content of fat up to 50% of the total calories.

(2) This aims to reduce the amount of C02 produced to decrease the burden
of ventilation.

i. Hepatic failure formulas: These formulas have an increased quantity of
branched-chain amino acids and reduced aromatic amino acids .

C. Access for enteral nutrition
l . Nasoenteric tubes (nasogastric and nasoduodenal tubes)
a. Intragastric feeding permits bolus feeding due to the reservoir capacity of
the stomach.

b . Feeding the stomach results in stimulation of the biliary-pancreatic axis,
which is trophic for the small bowel.

c. Gastric secretions also have a dilutional euect on the osmolarity of the feed
ings , decreasing the incidence of diarrhea.

d. Nasogastric feeding should be administered to patients with intact mental
status and protective laryngeal reflexes to minimize the risk of aspiration.

e . Nasoduodenal feedings decrease the risk of aspiration pneumonia by 25%.

f. Placement of tubes past the pylorus is technically difficult. However,
fluoroscopic-guided placement has a high success rate.

## 2 . Percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG)

a. This technique is used for long-term enteral nutrition access.

b. Catheters are placed in the stomach percutaneously using endoscopic
guidance.

c. Relative contraindications to PEG include ascites, coagulopathy, gastric
varices, gastric neoplasm, and lack of suitable abdominal site.

3. Percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy-j ejunostomy (PEG-J) and direct percu
taneous endoscopic j ejunostomy (DPEJ)
a. These techniques are used for patients who cannot tolerate gastric feedings
or are at risk of aspiration.

b . PEG-] is performed by passing a tube past the pylorus into the j ejunum
using an existing PEG tube. This is done endoscopically or fluoroscopically.

PEG-] has a more than 50% malfunction rate due to retrograde tube migra
tion, clogging, and kinking.

D P E J h a s a lower rate of
m a l f u n ction than P E G - J but
is tec h n i c a l l y m u c h m o re
c h a l l e n g i n g .

c. DPEJ i s performed using the same techniques a s P E G b u t requires the
enteroscope to reach the j ejunum.

4. Surgical gastrostomy and j ejunostomy
D. Complications of enteral nutrition
l . Abdominal distension and cramps: This is managed by temporarily
discontinuing feeds and resuming at a lower infusion rate.

2. Pneumatosis intestinalis and small bowel necrosis
a. This occurs as a result of bowel distension and consequent reduction in
bowel wall perfusion.

b . Factors implicated include hyperosmolarity of tube feeds, bacterial
overgrowth, fermentation, and metabolic breakdown products.

c. Initiation of enteric tube feedings in critically ill patients should be
delayed until they have been adequately resuscitated so that an already
hypoperfused bowel is not stressed further.

d. Tube feeds can also be diluted, or solutions with low osmolarity can be
used so that less digestion is needed by the GI tract.

V. Parenteral Nutrition
A. General principles : Parenteral nutrition consists of infusion of a hyperosmolar
solution containing carbohydrates, proteins , fats, and other important nutrients.

B. Indications
l . Prolonged ileus (less than 7 to 10 days) after a major operation
2. Hypermetabolic patients in whom enteral nutrition is not possible or adequate
(e.g. , critically ill patients , cancer patients)
3. Short bowel syndrome
4. High-output enterocutaneous Hstulas (output > 500 mUday)

## 5 . Malabsorption (e.g. , pancreatic insufficiency, celiac disease, inflammatory

bowel disease)
6. Functional GI disorders (e.g. , esophageal dyskinesia, anorexia nervosa)
C. Routes of parenteral nutrition
l . TPN (also called central parenteral nutrition)
a. These solutions are hyperosmolar and must therefore be delivered into a
high-flow system (i. e . , a central vein) to prevent venous sclerosis.

b. A standard TPN solution contains 1 5% to 25% dextrose, 1 0% amino acid,
lipids and electrolytes, minerals, and vitamins.

c. Lipids are primarily in the form of long-chain triglycerides, which provide
essential fatty acids (linoleic acid) . However, the high content of these polyun
saturated fatty acids has harmful effects on pulmonary and immune function.

## 2 . Peripheral parenteral nutrition (PPN)

a. These solutions can be administered via peripheral veins because they have
low osmolarity, secondary to reduced levels of dextrose (5% to 1 0%) and
protein (3%) .

b. Some nutrients cannot be administered due to inability to concentrate them
into small volumes.

c. Typically, PPN is used for nutritional support for short periods (less than
2 weeks) when central venous access is not available or feasible.

D . Complications of TPN
l . TPN is associated with more complications, compared with enteral feeding,
due to intestinal bacterial overgrowth and increased gut permeability, leading
to bacterial translocation. However, parenteral feeding still has fewer infectious
complications compared with no feeding at all.

2. Complications of TPN can be divided into mechanical, metabolic, and
infectious.

a. Mechanical: central line insertion-related complications
b. Metabolic
( 1 ) Hyperglycemia: Glucose should be monitored closely and maintained in a
normal range to minimize associated complications.

(2) Hypoglycemia: due to sudden cessation of TPN . Treated by administering
dextrose.

S U R G I C A L P H Y S I O L O G Y e 1 9
(3) Carbon dioxide retention: due to excess glucose administration. Treated by
decreasing glucose calories and replacing with fat.

(4) Azotemia: due to excess amino acid administration. Treated by decreasing
amino acids and increasing glucose calories.

(5) Hypertriglyceridemia: due to rapid fat infusion. Treated by decreasing rate
of fat infusion.

(6) Liver enzyme elevation: Mild elevation of transaminases, alkaline
phosphatase, and bilirubin may occur. However, this is transient, and
if liver enzymes do not plateau or return to normal over 7 to 14 days,
another cause of the enzyme elevation should be investigated. Excess
glucose is stored in the form of fat and results in hepatic steatosis.

Long-term TPN administration results in cholestasis and formation of
gallstones.

(7) Mineral, vitamin, and essential fatty acid deficiencies
c. Infectious: Sepsis can occur due to line infection or contamination of the
solution. TPN should be used cautiously among immunocompromised
patients .
```
